title: Chapter 11: Escalation Protocol
content: The auditor returned at dawn.
This time, it hadn't come alone.

Behind the featureless-suited figure stood two entities that made the Executioner look like a garden ornament. Nine feet tall. Bodies wrapped in shifting legal documents — pages of fine print crawling over their forms like living skin. They had no faces, no visible weapons. But the air around them had changed: colder, heavier, wrong.
"Ms. Vance," the auditor said, its smooth voice betraying nothing. "I've consulted with my supervisor."
"And?"
"Your counter-offer was... unconventional. My supervisor was not amused."
"Sorry to hear that."
"He sent the Enforcement Division instead."
The two entities stepped forward. Frost spread beneath their feet, crackling across the pavement.

"The audit will proceed. You will submit to identity verification, bloodline confirmation, and card authentication. Failure to comply will result in immediate seizure of the Primordial artifact and detention of its bearer."
Roxanne looked at Silas.
"Can you take them?"
"At current power levels? One. Maybe one and a half. The Enforcement Division is designed to handle rogue Primordials."
"So we can't fight."
"Not directly."
She looked at the card. Then at the entities. Then at the price tags floating above their heads.

*ENFORCER UNIT A — 5,000,000 OBOLS (BUYOUT)*
*ENFORCER UNIT B — 5,000,000 OBOLS (BUYOUT)*
*AUDITOR — 2,000,000 OBOLS (BUYOUT)*
"I'm not going to fight you."
The auditor's head tilted. "A wise decision."
"I'm going to buy you."
She held up the card.
*BULK PURCHASE: 2 ENFORCER UNITS + 1 AUDITOR. TOTAL: 12,000,000 OBOLS.*
The card pulsed.
*APPROVED.*
The two Enforcement entities stopped mid-stride. The frost receded. They turned their blank faces toward the auditor.
The auditor stood perfectly still.
"That's..."
"Buying your team out from under you?" Roxanne finished. "Yes. Yes, it is."
She pocketed the card.

"Now. You have a supervisor who needs a message. Tell him the Heir of the Primordial Void is not accepting audits today. Not any day. And if he sends another team, I'll buy them too. Then I'll come for him."
The auditor stood frozen for a long, silent moment.
Then it turned and walked away. The two Enforcement units fell into step behind Roxanne.
Silas watched them go.
"You just bought three Nether-Grid bureaucrats."
"I know."
"What are you going to do with them?"
"I have no idea. But I bet they're great at paperwork."
*End of Chapter 11*
The auditor stopped walking. Turned. Its featureless face regarded Roxanne with what might have been confusion.
"You purchased my Enforcement detail."
"I did."
"They are not for sale."
"Everything has a price. The card showed me theirs. I paid."
"The Enforcement Division is a regulatory body. It cannot be privately owned."
"It can now. By me."
She stepped closer. The two Enforcement units moved with her. They were hers now — she could feel their contract links, warm and certain, in the back of her consciousness.
"Here's how this is going to work. You're going to go back to your supervisor. You're going to tell him that the Heir of the Primordial Void is not accepting audits. Not today. Not ever."
"And if he doesn't accept that?"
"Then I'll buy his position. And his building. And his entire department. And I'll make him my assistant, and his first task will be reorganizing my new campus filing system."
The auditor stood motionless. The Nether-Grid's compliance ticker scrolled across its chest: *STATUS: REVIEWING.*
"You cannot buy a regulatory department."
"Watch me."
A long silence.
Then the auditor said:
"I will relay your message."
"Good. You're dismissed."
The auditor turned and walked away. The two Enforcement units stayed.
Roxanne turned to face them. Up close, they were even more imposing — nine feet of legal authority given physical form.
"You work for me now," she said. "First task: stand at the campus gates and look intimidating. Second task: if anything tries to hurt my people, stop it."
The units nodded. One of them raised a hand in what might have been a salute.
"Third task: learn to wave at children. The locals are scared enough."
The units exchanged a glance. Then, slowly, they both raised their hands.
Waved.
It was terrifying and adorable in roughly equal measure.
Silas appeared beside her, carrying two lattes.
"You bought the Enforcement Division."
"Two units of it."
"And taught them to wave."
"Gestural communication is important in any organization."
He handed her a latte. "You're building an army."
"I'm building a city. Armies are cheaper."
She took a sip. The foam art was a tiny galaxy, swirling with stars.
"Did you put a galaxy in my latte?"
"It seemed appropriate."
The Exchange had a rhythm. Roxanne was learning to feel it.
Every transaction created a ripple. Every contract signed shifted the balance somewhere. The whole system was an enormous, interconnected web of debt and credit, and she was now a node at its center.
"We need to move faster," she said, walking through the campus. "The Council will regroup. They'll find a loophole. We need to secure more territory, more resources, more leverage before they do."
"You can't buy everything at once," Silas said.
"Watch me."
She held up the card.
*DISPLAY: ALL AVAILABLE DEBT CONTRACTS — EASTERN SECTOR.*
The screen filled with thousands of entries. Personal debts. Business debts. Soul contracts. Each with a buyout price, each representing a person or entity bound to the old system.
*TOTAL: 47,293 CONTRACTS. TOTAL BUYOUT: 12,400,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase all."
*APPROVED. 12,400,000 OBOLS DEBITED. 47,293 CONTRACTS FORGIVEN.*
The Exchange feed updated. A wave of green icons spread across the map — debts cleared, obligations dissolved.
All across the city, people felt it. A weight lifting. A chain breaking. A sudden, inexplicable freedom.
Roxanne pocketed the card.
"That's a start."
The Enforcement units settled into their posts faster than Roxanne expected.

Within an hour, they had established patrol routes, a shift schedule, and a communication protocol. They did it without being asked. They were, Roxanne realized, professionals. The kind of professionals who had been doing this job for centuries.
"They're efficient," Silas observed.
"They're trained. There's a difference."
"Is there?"

"Efficiency is doing things right. Training is doing things the way you were taught. Right now they're doing things the way they were taught by the Council. I need to make sure they're doing things my way."
She walked over to the unit stationed at the main gate. The one that had saluted.
"You spoke earlier. What's your designation?"
"Unit E-7, ma'am."
"E-7. Not very personal."
"We don't require personal designations, ma'am."
"I'm giving you one anyway. From now on, you're 'Sev.'"
"That's... not standard procedure."
"Nothing I do is standard procedure. Welcome to the team, Sev."
